risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was conducted using a block randomized design with blocks of 4 and 6. The 0 and 1 within each block were random and the order of the group of 4 and the group of 6 was random." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation sequence with block randomization and random ordering within blocks, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Randomization occurred after each participant completed the pretest questionnaire... individuals were assigned to the next condition on the randomization table as they completed the questionnaire." Comment: The paper explicitly states that assignment occurred only after baseline completion and was based on a pre-prepared randomization table. While it does not mention central allocation or sealed envelopes, the online questionnaire site being checked daily and participants being assigned sequentially to the next condition suggests the recruiter could not foresee the upcoming assignment before enrollment, so this is the best-supported judgement as low risk for concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Once randomization was complete, participants were notified of the condition to which they were assigned (no blinding procedures were employed)" and "For the experimental group, the emails included a reminder to use the program. For the control group, the email included information about when the second questionnaire would be available." Comment: Participants and personnel were explicitly not blinded, and the outcomes were self-reported stress, distress, coping, satisfaction, and related behaviors that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation and differential encouragement/support, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Once randomization was complete, participants were notified of the condition to which they were assigned (no blinding procedures were employed)" and "The 30-minute online self-report questionnaire contained the following measures" / "Results of the regression analyses using the imputed data testing the differential improvement on the self-reported outcome measures." Comment: The outcome assessor was the patient/participant themselves (self-report questionnaires), and participants were explicitly unblinded. Because the main outcomes were subjective self-reported stress and related measures, knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement, so detection bias is high risk.

Quote: "There were fourteen participants who did not respond at posttest. Of these participants, thirteen were in the treatment group, representing a significant difference between conditions. While the overall attrition rate was 13.4% (14/104), the experimental group attrition rate was 25% (13/52)." Comment: Attrition was clearly imbalanced, with substantially more missing posttest data in the intervention arm and the authors explicitly note a significant difference between conditions. Although they used multiple imputation and ITT, the missingness was differential and could plausibly bias the treatment effect, so High risk is appropriate.
